JavaScript example 0.
Goal: teach how to parse input with a loop.
The example uses a array and a Array value.
```js
function process_javascript_0_0(items) {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_0([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a array and a Array value.
```js
function process_javascript_0_42(items) {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_42([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```js
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_461([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```

A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.

```js
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_545([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```

The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.

The example uses a object and a Array value.
```js
function process_javascript_0_629(items) {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_629([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```js
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_837([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```

A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.

```js
function process_javascript_0_1210(items) {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_1210([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```js
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_1251([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a map and a Array value.

```js
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
```

JavaScript example 1416.
Goal: teach how to parse input with a retry step.

```js

  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
```

```js
function process_javascript_0_1499(items) {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_1499([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```js
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_1540([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

Goal: teach how to count repeated values with a small class.
The example uses a map and a number value.
```js
function process_javascript_0_1623(items) {
  let total = 0;
  for (const value of items) {
    if (value >= 0) total += value;
  }
  return total;
}

console.assert(process_javascript_0_1623([1, -2, 3]) === 4);
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is TypeError.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.
